title: Distilling Neuro-Symbolic Programs into 3D Multi-modal LLMs
abstract:
Current 3D spatial reasoning methods face a fundamental trade-off: neuro-symbolic 3D (NS3D) concept learners achieve interpretable reasoning through compositional programs but are constrained to closed-set concept vocabularies and simple programs; end-to-end 3D multi-modal LLMs (3D MLLMs) could handle complex natural language and open-vocabulary concepts but suffer from black-box reasoning without explicit spatial verification.
We introduce APEIRIA, a neuro-symbolic 3D MLLM to bridge two paradigms by distilling symbolic reasoning patterns into MLLMs with natural language chain-of-thought. 
Our three-stage curriculum progressively builds reasoning capabilities:
a) 3D perception alignment grounds object visual-geometric features to the LLM, 
b) CoT-SFT teaches query decomposition and stepwise verification from symbolic program traces, 
and c) CoT-RL extends reasoning patterns to open-set concepts and deeply nested instructions.
By transferring reasoning patterns rather than concept-specific knowledge, APEIRIA preserves key NS3D virtues: transparent reasoning and modular interchangeability of planning and perception components.
Evaluations on grounding, question answering, and captioning show that APEIRIA surpasses prior NS3D methods and matches state-of-the-art 3D MLLMs on 3D spatial reasoning datasets, unifying symbolic methods' systematic reasoning with MLLMs' flexibility. 
Code is available at https://github.com/oceanflowlab/APEIRIA.
decision_comment:
The paper introduces the APEIRIA framework, which enhances reasoning in 3D vision-language models by distilling structured neuro-symbolic reasoning.

The main concerns raised include: limited evaluation benchmarks (2D4M); scalability issues of the neuro-symbolic representation (arig, A5U3, 7qpB); limitations in the reward design (2D4M, arig); unclear presentation of certain components (2D4M, A5U3, 7qpB); and questions regarding the level of novelty (2D4M).

The rebuttal addressed most of these concerns, and all reviewers recommended a weak accept. The AC concurs with this assessment.

reviews.summary: To address issues of transparency in MLLM reasoning and the limited capabilities of the neuro-symbolic framework in an open-vocabulary setting, the authors proposed the APEIRIA framework (neuron-symbolic 3D MLLMs). This framework addresses these two main problems by training the LLM with CoT generated via neuro-symbolic execution, which ensures the LLM's reasoning is transparent and beyond the black box of the baseline model. The training phase includes three stages: Perception Alignment, Symbolic Injection, and Open-Set and Complex Reasoning Generalization. The perception alignment trains the model to comprehend the basics of 3D spatial understanding. The Symbolic Injection is the supervised fine-tuning stage that uses supervision from Chain-of-Thought reasoning generated by a symbolic program.  Lastly, the Open-Set and Complex Reasoning Generalization stage uses GRPO to help the model learn more about spatial grounding via the Soft Grounding Reward function. The APEIRIA framework demonstrates effectiveness on two 3D vision-language benchmarks (ScanRefer and Multi3DRefer) against several neuro-symbolic and 3D MLLM baselines.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
# Strengths

- The proposed framework illustrates effectiveness across several SOTA models, including Neuro-Symbolic models, Specialist Methods, and even 3D MLLMs.

- The authors provide comprehensive ablation studies to ensure that each proposed component provides significant value to the whole proposed framework.

- The proposed model illustrates the improvement when increasing the parameter sizes, which is promising for extending the method to even larger models. 

- The problems that the proposed framework attempted to address are significant and worth exploring, especially the transparency of reasoning.

- The proposed framework is flexible. Some components, i.e., the perception and planning components, can be replaced if a more advanced method is developed in the future, potentially enhancing the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

- The paper is well written and includes some illustrations to help audiences understand the methodology.


---


# Weaknesses

- The evaluation is limited to only two benchmarks. Currently, several 3D Spatial Reasoning datasets have been developed and may provide more in-depth analysis for the framework. For example 3DSRBench[1], OMNISPATIAL[2]. Extending the evaluation to the newly developed benchmarks, which use a more comprehensive task taxonomy, might enhance the paper's effectiveness and provide a more thorough analysis of the model.

- The rewards function of GRPO only focuses on Soft-Grouning Rewards and Format Reward. There is no reward for the neuro-symbolic part, which may be important for ensuring the transparency of the model's generated reasoning trace.

- The pipeline of the APEIRIA framework is unclear during inference time. The example provided in the paper shows a reasoning trace generated purely by the model; however, the author mentions a perception replacement that required access to the generated symbolic instruction. If the model performs all reasoning on its own, this could undermine the claim to have created a model that provides a transparent reasoning trace, which is significant for the paper. Providing an example, even in the appendix of the actual APERIA pipeline, could help resolve this.

- The novelty of the proposed framework seems to be limited. The main novelty of the paper lies in generating Chain-of-Thought reasoning from neuro-symbolic representations. The training stages are widely used, especially for SFT and GRPO.

- The proposed Soft Grounding rewards, while showing the improvement over the IoU rewards, the improvement seems negligible.


[1] Ma, W., et al. (2024). 3DSRBench: A Comprehensive 3D Spatial Reasoning Benchmark. 

[2] Jia, M., et al. (2025). OmniSpatial: Towards Comprehensive Spatial Reasoning Benchmark for Vision Language Models.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. Why do authors limit the evaluation to only two benchmarks? Do the authors plan to include more recent 3D spatial reasoning benchmarks to provide a more in-depth analysis of the proposed framework using the recently developed benchmark?

2. Can the generated response from APEIRIA be used in neuro-symbolic execution, and can the generated neuro-symbolic response be verified for the correctness of the reasoning trace?
reviews.limitations: Yes, the author includes the limitations section.
reviews.final_justification: Rebuttal addressed my initial concerns regarding the justification of novelty, verification of reasoning trace, and clearer explanation of some unclear parts.

reviews.summary: This paper studies 3D visual grounding by trying to combine the interpretability of neuro-symbolic methods with the flexibility of 3D MLLMs. The main idea is to distill symbolic programs into natural language chain-of-thought traces, then further improve generalization with RL.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength 1. Clear and well-motivated idea.

The paper tackles a real limitation in current 3D grounding methods: symbolic models are interpretable but rigid, while MLLM-based methods are flexible but often unreliable in spatial reasoning. Distilling symbolic execution into CoT is a sensible and interesting way to connect the two.

Strength 2. Performance is good. 

The paper compared its method with various previous baselines, and provide comprehensive ablation study. Which verified the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Weakness 1.
The method relies heavily on synthetic symbolic supervision. The Stage 2 training signal appears to be crucial, but it comes from datasets with strong programmatic structure and fully specified intermediate supervision. This raises concerns about scalability: transferring the method to new domains may require substantial manual effort to design symbolic grammars, parsing rules, and data construction pipelines.

Weakness 2. 
The reward design is too heuristic and maybe only suitable for this specific scenario, can it be more generalized or simple for general scenarios?

reviews.summary: This paper introduces APEIRIA, a framework that enforces planning and execution in neuro-symbolic style on MLLM thinking process. APEIRIA uses a three-stage pipeline that gradually enforces object-centric understanding, neuro-symbolic enforced CoT and complex reasoning. Extensive experiments on ScanRefer, Multi3DRefer, Nr3D and Sr3D and ablation studies of the different components of the pipeline, including training stages and modules, have demonstrated the robustness and efficacy of the proposed framework.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper elicits explicit neuro-symbolic reasoning into 3D MLLM, which benefits the MLLM reasoning capability.

2. The paper conducts extensive experiments to validate the idea, including experiments on different benchmarks and extensive ablation experiments.

3. The method shows strong generalizability by showing the capability of developing new predicates.

Weaknesses:

1. The RL stage (Stage 3) seems to be not connected to the proposed neuro-symbolic framework, additional explanation of why introducing this stage should be added.

2. It is unclear when ablating the RL stage for ScanRefer and Multi3DRefer, whether it is removing the corresponding training dataset, or removing the entire RL stage.

3. Since the method is explicitly trained on ScanRefer and Multi3DRefer training set, how well will it generalize remains unclear, i.e. can it still performs well when trained on ScanRefer but tested on Multi3DRefer? The generalization experiment using only Sr3D and Nr3D seems to be not strong enough since they're from the same set of 3D scenes.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: Please see the Weakness section. In addition, I would prefer an explicit statement of what training data are used for each stage in the main text instead of the appendix.
reviews.limitations: 1. Since the method distills neuro-symbolic style reasoning into CoT of the LLM, how strict the LLM will follow such pattern remains unclear.
reviews.final_justification: I believe the additional experiments and clarification makes the work more solid. I'll maintain my positive score.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a chain-of-thought (CoT) enhanced 3D vision-language model by distilling structured neuro-symbolic reasoning into a 3D VLM. Specifically, the authors first construct a neural-symbolic reasoning framework that converts 3D scene understanding tasks into structured, formatted reasoning traces, enabling explicit intermediate logical steps grounded in 3D spatial representations. These formatted reasoning texts are then used to supervise the training of a 3D vision-language model following a reasoning-oriented paradigm: the model is first fine-tuned with CoT-style supervised learning (CoT-SFT) and subsequently optimized using reinforcement learning (GRPO) to further improve reasoning consistency and answer correctness. Through this two-stage training pipeline, the model learns to generate interpretable reasoning chains for 3D spatial tasks. Experiments on multiple 3D reasoning and embodied understanding benchmarks demonstrate improved reasoning accuracy and enhanced generalization compared to standard 3D VLM training strategies.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Strengths**

1. Structured reasoning for 3D understanding.
The paper introduces a neuro-symbolic framework that incorporates structured intermediate reasoning steps into 3D vision-language modeling. This design explicitly bridges symbolic scene representations with neural generative models, providing a principled way to inject structured reasoning into 3D understanding.

2. Interpretable and explainable reasoning traces.
By supervising the model with formatted reasoning chains grounded in scene graphs, the approach enables interpretable multi-step explanations for 3D spatial tasks. This improves transparency compared to standard end-to-end 3D VLM training.

3. Clear and well-organized presentation.
The paper is clearly written, with a well-motivated problem formulation and a logically structured description of the training pipeline (CoT-SFT followed by GRPO), making the overall methodology easy to follow.

**Weakness**
1. Limited expressivity of the symbolic 3D representation.
The proposed neuro-symbolic supervision is built upon the Symbolic-3D representation described in Appendix A, which appears to contain a relatively limited set of predefined spatial relations and attributes. Although the vocabulary is open-ended at the language level, the underlying symbolic schema seems restricted to a fixed relational set. This may constrain the expressive capacity of the reasoning space and limit the model to graph-level relational reasoning rather than richer geometric, continuous, or physics-aware 3D reasoning.

2. Scalability and dependence on structured symbolic annotations.
The structured reasoning traces are derived from existing symbolic 3D scene representations and reformatted into chain-of-thought style supervision. While this ensures correctness and interpretability, it raises questions about scalability and generalization to new domains. In contrast to natural language CoT data (which is already scarce in domains like mathematical reasoning), the proposed approach appears to rely on the availability of high-quality symbolic scene graphs. It is unclear how the framework would extend to settings where such structured representations are unavailable or noisy.

3. Modular Inference Enhancement and external model reliance.
The Modular Inference Enhancement stage leverages additional model capabilities during inference. While this improves performance, it introduces ambiguity regarding where the reasoning capability truly resides. Although 3D-MLLMs may not directly support plug-and-play integration of such modules, it remains unclear whether comparable enhancements could also benefit other baseline architectures. A more controlled comparison would help disentangle improvements due to structured reasoning training from gains obtained via external modular components.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
The questions below are corresponding to the weaknesses. 
1. Can we phase all 3d-spatial tasks in format of symbolics reasoning in this paper? If no, what will be the failure case.
2. How can we constructs a large number of reasoning traces in symbolic format, especially in a new scene such as outdoor navigation tasks.
3. Is Modular Inference Enhancement appliable to traditional Neuro-symbolic methods or specialist methods?
reviews.final_justification: This is overall a good paper with a clear presentation, and I choose to keep the positive score after the rebuttal phase. During the rebuttal, my questions about the neural symbolic dataset and the implementation were resolved. I think this paper fits the requirements of ICML and suggests a score of 4.
